meta.question: Do eggs increase cardiovascular disease risk?

positions.label: No increased risk / possibly lower risk

factors.label: Judge eggs on biomarkers (LDL) vs hard CVD events
factors.rationale: Weights hard clinical endpoints from large pooled cohorts.

provenance.quote: Conclusions People with prediabetes or T2D who consumed a 3-mo high-egg weight-loss diet with a 6-mo follow-up exhibited no adverse changes in cardiometabolic markers compared with those who consumed a low-egg weight-loss diet.

This pooled cohort study reports that consumption of an additional 300 mg of dietary cholesterol per day or an additional half egg per day is significantly associated with higher risk of incident cardiovascular disease (CVD) events and all-cause mortality.
Carotid plaque area increased linearly with age after age 40, but increased exponentially with pack-years of smoking and with egg-yolk years.

The level of evidence was very weak for all the significant associations: risk of heart failure (RR 1.15; 95%CI: 1.02-1.30), cancer mortality (RR 1.13; 95%CI 1.06-1.20), higher levels of LDL cholesterol (WMD 7.39; 95%CI 5.82-8.95), total cholesterol (WMD 9.12; 95%CI 7.35-10.89), and apolipoprotein B-100 (WMD 0.06; 95%CI 0.03-0.08).

factors.label: Discount for healthy-user / dietary-pattern confounding
factors.rationale: Claims the association survives confounding adjustment.

provenance.quote: Study limitations include its observational nature, reliance on participant self-report, and residual confounding despite extensive adjustment for acknowledged dietary and lifestyle risk factors.

After adjustment for age, smoking, and other potential CHD risk factors, we found no evidence of an overall significant association between egg consumption and risk of CHD or stroke in either men or women.

factors.label: Population/context dependence (who is eating eggs)
factors.rationale: A different population and background diet flips the direction of association.

provenance.quote: Conclusions People with prediabetes or T2D who consumed a 3-mo high-egg weight-loss diet with a 6-mo follow-up exhibited no adverse changes in cardiometabolic markers compared with those who consumed a low-egg weight-loss diet.

* This prospective cohort study of the general Chinese population demonstrated a significantly inverse association of egg consumption with CVD, ischaemic heart disease, haemorrhagic stroke, ischaemic stroke, as well as major coronary events.
The wide range of outcomes, with substantial variability and high heterogeneity, indicated a lack of robust evidence.

factors.label: Subgroups (diabetics / hyper-responders) differ
factors.rationale: Overall null hides a diabetic subgroup signal — the crux of 'depends on who'.

provenance.quote: The apparent increased risk of CHD associated with higher egg consumption among diabetic participants warrants further research.
Egg consumption was associated with an elevated risk of incident type 2 diabetes.

factors.label: Industry funding as a source-quality concern
factors.rationale: A systematic review found that industry-funded egg-intervention papers were substantially more likely to describe cholesterol increases favorably, so the caution camp treats funding as a material interpretation risk.

provenance.quote: However, 49% of industry-funded intervention studies reported conclusions that were discordant with study results (ie, net cholesterol increases were described as favorable in the articles' stated conclusions), compared with 13% of non-industry-funded studies.

contextSources.title: Barnard et al. — Industry Funding and Cholesterol Research: A Systematic Review

position.quote: However, 49% of industry-funded intervention studies reported conclusions that were discordant with study results (ie, net cholesterol increases were described as favorable in the articles' stated conclusions), compared with 13% of non-industry-funded studies.

contextSources.role: factor-only; excluded from position and independence counts

sources.title: Zhong et al. — Associations of dietary cholesterol/egg consumption with CVD and mortality (JAMA)

position.quote: Meaning Among US adults, higher consumption of dietary cholesterol or eggs was significantly associated with higher risk of incident CVD and all-cause mortality in a dose-response manner.

sources.title: Zhuang et al. — Egg & cholesterol consumption and mortality (NIH-AARP, PLOS Medicine)

position.quote: In this study, intakes of eggs and cholesterol were associated with higher all-cause, CVD, and cancer mortality.

fundingDetails: National Key Research and Development Program of China

sources.title: Drouin-Chartier et al. — Egg consumption and risk of cardiovascular disease: three cohorts + meta-analysis (BMJ)

position.quote: The results from our cohort study and updated meta-analysis show that moderate egg consumption (up to one egg per day) is not associated with cardiovascular disease risk overall.

sources.title: Dehghan et al. — Egg consumption and CVD/mortality in 50 countries (PURE)
sources.venue: The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition

position.quote: CONCLUSIONS In 3 large international prospective studies including ∼177,000 individuals, 12,701 deaths, and 13,658 CVD events from 50 countries in 6 continents, we did not find significant associations between egg intake and blood lipids, mortality, or major CVD events.

sources.title: Qin et al. — Egg consumption and cardiovascular disease (China Kadoorie Biobank)

position.quote: Compared with non-consumers, daily egg consumption was associated with lower risk of CVD (HR 0.89, 95% CI 0.87 to 0.92).

sources.title: Hu et al. — A prospective study of egg consumption and risk of cardiovascular disease (JAMA)

position.quote: These findings suggest that consumption of up to 1 egg per day is unlikely to have substantial overall impact on the risk of CHD or stroke among healthy men and women.

sources.title: Rong et al. — Egg consumption and risk of CHD and stroke: dose-response meta-analysis (BMJ)

position.quote: **Conclusions** Higher consumption of eggs (up to one egg per day) is not associated with increased risk of coronary heart disease or stroke.

sources.title: Fuller et al. (DIABEGG) — High vs low egg diet in type 2 diabetes: randomized trial
sources.venue: The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition

position.quote: Conclusions People with prediabetes or T2D who consumed a 3-mo high-egg weight-loss diet with a 6-mo follow-up exhibited no adverse changes in cardiometabolic markers compared with those who consumed a low-egg weight-loss diet.

sources.title: Blesso & Fernandez — Whole egg consumption improves lipoprotein profiles (feeding trial)

position.quote: Conclusions Incorporating daily whole egg intake into a moderately carbohydrate-restricted diet provides further improvements in the atherogenic lipoprotein profile and in insulin resistance in individuals with MetS.

sources.title: Carson et al. — Dietary cholesterol and cardiovascular risk: an AHA science advisory

position.quote: Dietary guidance should focus on healthy dietary patterns (eg, Mediterranean-style and DASH [Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension]-style diets) that are inherently relatively low in cholesterol with typical levels similar to the current US intake.

sources.title: Djoussé & Gaziano — Egg consumption and risk of heart failure (Physicians' Health Study)

position.quote: However, egg consumption of > or = 1 per day is related to an increased risk of HF among US male physicians.

sources.title: Spence, Jenkins & Davignon — Egg yolk consumption and carotid plaque

position.quote: Interpretation Our findings suggest that regular consumption of egg yolk should be avoided by persons at risk of cardiovascular disease.

sources.title: Alexander et al. — Meta-analysis of egg consumption and risk of CHD and stroke
sources.venue: Journal of the American College of Nutrition

position.quote: Based on the results of this meta-analysis, consumption of up to one egg daily may contribute to a decreased risk of total stroke, and daily egg intake does not appear to be associated with risk of CHD.Key Teaching Points:• The role of egg consumption in the risk of stroke and coronary heart disease has come under scrutiny over many years.• A comprehensive meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies that reported risk estimates for egg consumption in association with CHD or stroke was performed on the peer-reviewed epidemiologic literature through August 2015.• Overall, summary associations indicate that intake of up to 1 egg daily may be associated with reduced risk of total stroke.• Overall, summary associations show no clear association between egg intake and increased or decreased risk of CHD.• Eggs are a relatively low-cost and nutrient-dense whole food that provides a valuable source of protein, essential fatty acids, antioxidants, choline, vitamins, and minerals.

sources.title: Tran et al. — Egg consumption and cardiovascular disease: a meta-analysis
sources.venue: Handbook of Egg Science and Technology

position.quote: Four of the six studies that examined CVD and mortality and egg consumption among diabetics found a statistically significant association.

sources.title: Virtanen et al. — Egg consumption and risk of coronary artery disease (Kuopio, KIHD)
sources.venue: The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition

position.quote: Conclusion Egg or cholesterol intakes were not associated with increased CAD risk, even in ApoE4 carriers (i.e., in highly susceptible individuals).

sources.title: Berger et al. — Dietary cholesterol and cardiovascular disease: a systematic review and meta-analysis
sources.venue: The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition

position.quote: Dietary cholesterol was not statistically significantly associated with any coronary artery disease (4 cohorts; no summary RR), ischemic stroke (4 cohorts; summary RR: 1.13; 95% CI: 0.99, 1.28), or hemorrhagic stroke (3 cohorts; summary RR: 1.09; 95% CI: 0.79, 1.50).

fundingDetails: American Egg Board, Egg Nutrition Center

sources.title: Shin et al. — Egg consumption in relation to cardiovascular disease and diabetes: a meta-analysis
sources.venue: The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition

position.quote: However, egg consumption may be associated with an increased incidence of type 2 diabetes among the general population and CVD comorbidity among diabetic patients.

sources.title: Larsson et al. — Egg consumption and risk of heart failure, myocardial infarction and stroke (Swedish cohorts)
sources.venue: The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition

position.quote: Consumption of eggs ≥1 time/d, but not less frequent consumption, was associated with an elevated risk of HF in men.

fundingDetails: Swedish Research Council for Health, Working Life and Welfare

sources.title: Godos et al. — Egg consumption and cardiovascular risk: a dose-response meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies

position.quote: There is no conclusive evidence on the role of egg in CVD risk, despite the fact that higher quality studies are warranted to obtain stronger evidence for a possible protection of CVD associated with moderate weekly egg consumption compared to no intake; equally, future studies may strengthen the evidence for increased heart failure risk associated with high regular egg consumption.

sources.title: Formisano et al. — Effect of egg consumption on health outcomes: an updated umbrella review

position.quote: No evidence of association was found among all cardiovascular outcomes and all-cause mortality risk between high vs. low egg consumption.
